appeal_letter:
Re: Appeal of denied prior authorization for Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) for severe Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (F42.2)

To the Appeals Department,

This letter appeals the denial of the prior authorization request for Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) submitted by my treating provider on October 5, 2026 for the treatment of severe Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (F42.2).

The denial rests on two stated grounds. First, the determination states that the submitted clinical notes did not sufficiently document the failure of two distinct classes of medication, which is described as a prerequisite under Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin 0469 to establish medical necessity for TMS. Second, the request was administratively closed and denied because the peer-to-peer discussion was not completed within the 5-business-day window, which the letter states expired on October 12, 2026.

I address both grounds directly, because each is the basis the denial itself relied on.

On the documentation of medication failure: the clinical record establishes that two distinct pharmacological classes were tried and failed at therapeutic dosages. The treating psychiatrist initiated an extended trial of fluvoxamine titrated to 300 mg/day beginning in March 2023, and after this failed to yield significant improvement transitioned to clomipramine, a tricyclic antidepressant, in November 2024. Both trials lasted over twelve weeks at therapeutic dosages, and the Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS) scores remained persistently elevated at 28 and 31 across two consecutive quarterly assessments. These represent two distinct medication classes. The clinical notes detailing these specific trials were included in the initial October 2026 authorization request, so the criterion the denial cites is in fact satisfied by the record that was submitted. In addition, the patient has participated in intensive Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) therapy since early 2025, and her severe OCD has remained refractory to these standard interventions, which is why the treating provider determined that TMS is the clinically appropriate next step.

On the administrative closure for a missed peer-to-peer window: the closure was the result of circumstances outside the provider's control. The provider's office made three documented attempts to schedule the required peer-to-peer review within the five-business-day window. The insurer's scheduling portal experienced a documented outage, and phone representatives were unable to provide an appointment time before the administrative deadline. Where the provider attempted in good faith to complete the discussion and was prevented by the insurer's own system, denying the request on the basis of the unmet window is not a fair application of that procedural guideline to the documented facts. The individualized facts here warrant completing the clinical review rather than closing the request on procedural grounds.

Requested action: please overturn the denial and approve the prior authorization for TMS. In the alternative, please reopen the request, schedule the peer-to-peer review, and complete a full and fair clinical review of the medical necessity for TMS based on the complete documented medication history and treatment outcomes already submitted.

Not legal or medical advice. Draft assistance only.

Respectfully,
Member
missing_evidence_checklist: Copies of the original October 2026 clinical notes documenting the fluvoxamine and clomipramine trials, dosages, durations, and Y-BOCS scores, as submitted with the authorization request
Provider letter of medical necessity confirming both medication classes failed and that TMS is the appropriate next step
Dated records of the three peer-to-peer scheduling attempts (call logs, portal screenshots, or confirmation references)
Any documentation of the scheduling portal outage and the dates and times affected
Records of ERP therapy participation since early 2025 and its outcomes
The denial letter date, to confirm the 180-day appeal filing deadline
